articles.title: The Audit You Cannot Argue With
articles.dek: Paul's sweeping verdict on all humanity isn't the last word — but it has to be the first.
articles.quote: You cannot receive grace you don't think you need.
articles.body:
In 2014, Satya Nadella inherited a company in quiet crisis. Microsoft had spent a decade fighting the wrong battles — suing competitors, building walls between internal teams, watching younger companies rewrite the technology map while they argued over turf. When Nadella took the CEO chair, one of his first moves was to commission a thorough, uncomfortable audit of the company's culture. Not the kind that sits in a binder. An honest reckoning: What are we, really, when no one is performing for the annual review?

The findings were not flattering. Managers were hoarding information. Engineers were undercutting each other to look good. The company had drifted, in Nadella's own phrase, into a culture of "know-it-alls" — people so invested in appearing right that they had stopped trying to become better. Nadella did not bury the report. He circulated it. He stood before thousands of employees and said plainly: this is who we have become, and it must change.

That audit did not transform Microsoft on its own. But it did something more important first: it made the problem undeniable. The diagnosis had to come before the cure — and that order was not an accident.

Romans 3:9–20 is Paul's audit of the entire human race.

He has spent two full chapters building the case. In chapter one, he catalogued the Gentile world's moral collapse — idolatry, cruelty, dishonesty. His Jewish readers likely nodded along. Yes, those people. Then chapter two reversed direction without warning. The Jews were not exempt: knowing the law and keeping it are not the same thing. Circumcision of the heart matters more than circumcision of the flesh. Heritage does not equal righteousness.

And now, in chapter three, Paul brings both groups into the same courtroom and reads the verdict aloud.

"What shall we conclude then? Do we have any advantage? Not at all! For we have already made the charge that Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin." (Romans 3:9, NIV)

Not some. Not most. All. Under the power of sin.

What follows is startling. Paul does not reach for his own accusations. He calls the Jewish scriptures themselves as witnesses against the Jewish people — psalms they memorized as children, passages sung at Passover, texts traced by devoted fingers on ancient scrolls. He lines them up one after another:

"There is no one righteous, not even one; there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God." (Romans 3:10–11, NIV)

"Their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit. The poison of vipers is on their lips." (Romans 3:13, NIV)

"Their feet are swift to shed blood; ruin and misery mark their ways, and the way of peace they do not know." (Romans 3:15–17, NIV)

This is devastating — not because Paul is cruel, but because he is quoting from six different psalms and the prophet Isaiah. These are Israel's own hymns. The very texts that were supposed to mark out God's people as special are now testifying against them. The advantage they thought they had — being the keepers of God's Word — becomes the evidence that seals the indictment. It is like a company's founding charter being read aloud in court, not to celebrate what it built, but to show precisely how far it had drifted from its own stated promise.

Paul is not introducing foreign accusations. He is holding up Israel's own mirror and saying: look at what you already knew.

And then comes the verdict:

"Therefore no one will be declared righteous in God's sight by the works of the law; rather, through the law we become conscious of our sin." (Romans 3:20, NIV)

Notice carefully what Paul says the law does. It does not save. It diagnoses.

Think about what a blood test does. It does not lower your cholesterol or clear your arteries. What it does is produce a number — a precise, undeniable reading — that tells you what is happening inside you whether you feel it or not. It makes the invisible visible. Without that number, you remain untreated because you remain unaware. The test is not the cure. It is what makes the cure possible.

That is exactly what Paul assigns to the law in verse 20. The commandments, the moral standards, the whole ethical framework of Scripture — never meant to generate righteousness from within us. Meant to show us that we cannot generate it at all. Meant to bring us to the end of every negotiation we have been running with our own conscience. You cannot receive grace you don't think you need. You cannot turn toward a rescue you haven't admitted you require.

Nadella's unflinching audit of Microsoft did not end in shame and shutdown. It became the foundation of one of the most remarkable corporate transformations in modern business history. Under his leadership, Microsoft's market value grew tenfold. What looked like the company's most vulnerable moment of self-honesty turned out to be the starting line of its greatest era. The diagnosis cleared the way for the cure — and only because the diagnosis was honest.

Paul is doing the same thing for the human soul. He lines up the evidence — all of it, from everyone — not because he enjoys the prosecution, but because something extraordinary is coming just two verses later: "But now apart from the law the righteousness of God has been made known." The "but now" requires the honest accounting of verses 9–20. Good news only lands with its full force if the honest news has been heard first.

So let this reach you where you are on an ordinary Tuesday. Maybe you have been running the usual internal negotiations: At least I'm not as bad as that person. I try hard, and that has to count. Look at everything I have given, everything I have sacrificed. All of that is deeply human. All of it is understandable. And all of it is a way of circling around the one honest place the text is gently pressing you toward: I need something I cannot produce for myself.

That is not defeat. That is the door.

Paul is not your adversary in these verses. He is your doctor, running the test that leads somewhere good. The diagnosis stings because it is accurate. Let it sting. Let the audit be thorough. Because on the other side of "no one righteous, not even one" is not condemnation — it is the announcement that someone else's righteousness has been freely made available to you. At no cost. On terms you did not earn and do not have to maintain.

You don't have to negotiate the audit results anymore. Set down the report and say simply: Yes. That's me. And then listen for what comes next.

The mirror doesn't dirty your face. It just shows you what was already there — so you can finally go wash.

articles.title: Open the Doors
articles.dek: Hezekiah's first act as king wasn't a speech or a strategy — it was a doorway. And what happened next took everyone by surprise.
articles.quote: The length of the neglect does not determine the speed of the restoration. What determines the speed is how quickly someone opens the doors.
articles.body:
In December 2024, the great doors of Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris swung open for the first time since fire had gutted the spire and scorched the nave five years before. Tens of thousands stood in the square outside. When the first notes of the rebuilt organ rang out through the restored stone, people who had not cried in years found themselves unable to stop.

A beloved place had been silent. Now it was singing again.

What stayed with so many observers was not only the grandeur of the restoration — it was the speed. Five years to rebuild what eight centuries had made. Stonemasons, glassworkers, and carpenters from across the world had worked through every season. When journalists asked how it was finished so quickly, the answer was almost always the same: We started immediately. We didn't wait for the perfect plan. We just opened the work.

That phrase kept returning to me as I read 2 Chronicles 29.

Hezekiah inherited a kingdom in ruins. Not a modest, manageable mess — a full, structural, generational kind of broken. His father Ahaz had been one of Judah's worst kings, and his most visible act of apostasy was precise and deliberate: he had physically shut the doors of the Lord's temple. He took the sacred vessels, cut them to pieces, locked the house of God, and moved on to other things (see 2 Chr 28:24). By the time Ahaz died, the temple in Jerusalem was dark, locked, and filling with debris. The lamps had gone out. The incense had stopped. The whole system of worship had ground to silence.

That was what greeted the twenty-five-year-old Hezekiah on his first day as king.

What he did next is worth reading slowly.

He did not spend his opening months establishing alliances, reassuring the court, or managing the optics of transition. He did not say, Let's give this a year to stabilize, then assess the temple situation. The chronicler is almost jarring in his directness: "In the first month of the first year of his reign, he opened the doors of the temple of the LORD and repaired them" (2 Chr 29:3).

First month. First year.

Then he gathered the priests and Levites — the ones whose vocation had been suspended, who had watched the temple go dark and were living in a kind of professional and spiritual limbo — and he spoke to them. Not from a throne. Not through an intermediary. He gathered them into the square on the east side and addressed them directly.

And here is the detail that stops me cold: he called them my sons.

Hezekiah was twenty-five. Some of these men were certainly older than he was. And yet — not my priests, not my servants, not even my people. "My sons, do not be negligent now, for the LORD has chosen you to stand before him and serve him, to minister before him and to burn incense" (2 Chr 29:11).

It is an intimate, pastoral word. Not a king issuing orders. A man reminding other men of who they were when the doors were still open — appealing not to duty but to identity, not to obligation but to calling.

They responded. They consecrated themselves and began pulling out the accumulated debris from a locked and long-abandoned sanctuary. Sixteen days of labor. And on the sixteenth day of the first month, it was done. The altar was reconsecrated. The instruments were brought out. Burnt offerings went up, singing rose, the whole assembly bowed in worship. One month to undo years of silence.

And then comes the verse that is the quiet hinge of the whole chapter:

Hezekiah and all the people rejoiced at what God had brought about for his people, because it was done so quickly. (2 Chronicles 29:36)

They did not just rejoice that restoration had happened. They rejoiced at how fast it happened. The text carries a tone of astonishment — they had not anticipated this pace. Years of locked doors and silent worship, and all of it had turned in a single month.

We tend to believe the opposite. We assume that the longer something has been broken, the longer the repair will take. Years of distance in a relationship, years of drifted prayer, years of living at arm's length from your own calling — surely that takes years to undo.

But notice what this chapter quietly insists: the length of the neglect does not determine the speed of the restoration. What determines the speed is how quickly someone opens the doors.

Ahaz had kept those doors shut through years of deliberate unfaithfulness. Hezekiah opened them on day one. And once the doors were open, God moved faster than anyone had anticipated. That is not a small comfort. That is almost scandalously good news.

So here is the question worth sitting with.

Is there something you have kept the doors shut on? Not because you planned to — it just happened. A morning prayer that used to anchor your day and somewhere stopped. A relationship you have been meaning to address when the timing feels better. A spiritual practice you set aside during a hard season and never picked back up. A version of yourself — open, hungry, alive to God — that you have not been in a while, and you are not sure exactly when the shift happened.

Closed doors are very quiet. That is the thing about them.

Hezekiah did not wait until he felt ready, or until the political situation was stable, or until he had a complete plan. He opened the doors. Then he gathered the people around him — not as subjects but as sons — and he spoke to them like a human being about what they all knew was true.

This is what relational wisdom sometimes looks like: not a comprehensive program, but a first act. Not a grand strategy, but an open door. Not a policy, but a conversation where you call someone by the right name — not their job title, not their failure, but their calling.

If there is a door in your life that has been shut a little too long, today might be the first month of your first year.

You do not need to have it all worked out. Open the door. Say what needs to be said. Begin the thing that needs beginning. The debris will take some clearing — it always does — but the clearing goes faster than you think when willing hands are in it together.

Hezekiah was young, his kingdom was broken, and the inherited mess was not his fault. None of that stopped him from opening the doors on day one.

God is the God of quick reversals. He was that for Hezekiah. He can be that for you. The organ is waiting. It just needs someone to open the door.

articles.title: The Only Thing He Asked
articles.dek: In the heart of Psalm 81, God makes the most one-sided offer in all of Scripture — and all it requires is one posture.
articles.quote: God does not promise to fill a politely cracked-open mouth. He said: open wide.
articles.illustration: newborn breast crawl Swedish neonatal research
articles.body:
In the late 1980s, Swedish neonatal researchers documented something that quietly changed how hospitals around the world handled newborn care. They placed babies — minutes old, unwashed, eyes still adjusting — on their mothers' chests, skin to skin, and simply watched. No instruction was given to the infants, for obvious reasons. The staff stood back and let the camera roll.

What they observed was not random. Each baby followed the same sequence: a period of stillness, a gradual waking, small movements of the mouth, a rocking of the head. And then — before the infant had ever been fed, before it possessed any experience of hunger or milk or being held — it turned toward warmth and opened its mouth wide. The researchers called it the breast crawl: an inborn reflex, present from birth, that drives a newborn toward nourishment with no prior training at all. Their published findings shifted postpartum practices globally. But the deeper discovery underneath the discovery was this — a human being does not need to learn how to receive. We arrive in the world already wired for it. The opening comes first, before any knowledge of what the opening is for.

God, apparently, designed it that way on purpose.

Psalm 81 is a psalm for a feast day. Most scholars connect it to the Feast of Trumpets or the Feast of Tabernacles — one of the great annual celebrations when all of Israel made pilgrimage to Jerusalem, when the temple courts filled with music and the smell of offerings, when families arrived from days of travel to mark another year of God's faithfulness. The psalm opens with an invitation to joy: "Sing for joy to God our strength; shout aloud to the God of Jacob!" (Psalm 81:1, NIV). Harps, lyres, the trumpet's sound. It is, by design, a day of abundance.

But midway through the psalm, the voice changes. God Himself begins speaking, in the first person, directly. He calls His people to hear Him — "Hear me, my people, and I will warn you — if you would only listen to me, Israel!" (Psalm 81:8, NIV). He recalls who He has been: the One who drove out the foreign gods, who freed them from carrying bricks in Egypt's heat, who tested them and stayed with them at Meribah. "I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of Egypt." (Psalm 81:10a, NIV) And then, on this feast day, surrounded by remembrance and trumpets and the smell of the harvest offering, He says one thing that has no equal anywhere else in the Hebrew Bible:

"Open wide your mouth and I will fill it." — Psalm 81:10b (NIV)

Eight words. The most one-sided offer in Scripture.

The Hebrew verb for open wide is rachab — to be spacious, to be broad, to expand beyond the ordinary measure. It is the same root used to describe the Promised Land itself: the broad and spacious land of Exodus 3:8, overflowing with milk and honey. God is, in a single phrase, drawing two images together: the land He gave them and the posture He is asking of them. Become as open as the land I prepared for you. That wide. And I will fill the space. The filling is entirely His work. The expanding is entirely His gift. The only act required of Israel is the opening.

This is not a demand for religious performance. It is not a call to earn the feast by keeping every rule correctly. It is an invitation to a posture — the posture of a child who has not yet learned to distrust the one doing the feeding.

And here is where the psalm becomes quietly devastating. Verse 11: "But my people would not listen to me; Israel would not submit to me." (Psalm 81:11, NIV)

They would not open their mouths.

Not because God had proven unreliable. He had parted the sea for them. He had rained bread from the sky across forty years in the desert. He had brought water out of rock and fire to guide them through the night. The track record was immaculate. The feast was laid. The provision was present and waiting on a holy day. And still they would not open wide.

There is something in a stubborn heart that cannot bear the posture of receiving. The closed mouth is not merely indifference — it is a quiet declaration: I will feed myself. I will find my own path. I do not need to open to you. Self-sufficiency has a kind of dignity that dependence does not. Israel, again and again, preferred the dignity to the meal.

God's response — and this is the most sobering thing in the passage — is not fire from heaven. It is a release. "So I gave them over to their stubborn hearts to follow their own devices." (Psalm 81:12, NIV) He let them have what they chose: the closed mouth, the independent path, the self-managed life. The table was still set. The provision was still waiting at the end of the road they refused. But you cannot fill a mouth that will not open. And so God, with the grief of a parent watching a child push the meal away, simply stepped back and allowed the choosing.

The newborn in those Swedish hospital rooms knew nothing of this. It had no pride yet — no sense that opening wide might look vulnerable, no training in composure, no instinct for managing what it showed. It simply followed what it was made for. It turned toward warmth and opened before it knew what was waiting. And what it needed was already there.

We have learned things that newborn had not yet learned. We have learned that dependence can feel exposed. That prayer — real prayer, the honest kind — can feel like admitting you are not handling it. That asking too desperately, with too little composure, risks something. So we come to God carefully: measured requests, softened language, needs whittled down to a size that will not embarrass us if the answer is slow in coming. We come with a mostly-closed mouth and wonder, in quiet moments, why the feast never quite fills us.

God does not promise to fill a politely cracked-open mouth. He said: open wide.

Filipino life has trained many of us to be composed, to carry our needs quietly, to not impose on others with what we think we should be able to manage ourselves. That instinct is beautiful in a family. It builds community. But it can follow us directly into our prayer life, until we are bringing God the curated, reasonable version of what we need instead of the true one. He is not asking for the managed version. He is asking for the rachab version — spacious, broad, open all the way down.

What would that look like today? Perhaps it is the prayer you have been softening — the need you have not said at full volume because it sounds too large, too exposed, too much like losing control. Perhaps it is the request you stopped making because the silence stretched past what felt reasonable. Perhaps it is simply sitting still this morning, before the day fills in, and letting the space inside you be larger than your plan. Not the careful version. The real one.

The provision was prepared before you arrived. It was ready on the feast day in ancient Israel. It was ready in the desert mornings when bread fell before anyone thought to ask. It is ready now — waiting, as it has always waited, for the one posture it has always required.

Open wide. He promised to fill it. He said so on a celebration day thousands of years ago, at the very moment when everything was already in place, when all that was missing was the mouth willing to receive. He has not changed the offer since. He simply waits — not with impatience, but with the quiet certainty of the one who laid the feast — for the mouth that will finally open wide enough to let Him in.

articles.title: Hold Fast
articles.dek: You have received more instruction than you realize. The question has never been whether you heard it — it's whether you're still holding on.
articles.quote: Wisdom isn't just a set of principles. It's a relationship. And relationships have to be held.
articles.body:
Every morning before dawn, in the highland forests of Kaptagat, Kenya, Eliud Kipchoge runs. Not at the pace his legs want to run. Not at the pace the greatest marathoner alive could run. He runs at the pace his coach tells him to run.

Patrick Sang has coached Kipchoge for over two decades. In that time, Kipchoge has rewritten what distance running looks like — a sub-two-hour marathon, multiple world records, back-to-back Olympic gold medals in Rio and Tokyo. When journalists ask for the secret, Kipchoge answers with the same unhurried certainty every time: "I follow 100% what my coach says."

Not 80%. Not when it feels right. Not when he agrees. One hundred percent.

Early in Kipchoge's career, the young runner kept wanting to push harder in training — to prove how strong he was, to show what his legs could do. Sang held him back. Slowed him down. Kept him to the plan. Kipchoge submitted, and kept submitting. Not because he had no instincts of his own — he had extraordinary ones. But because he had decided that the instruction of a wise coach, faithfully held over time, was worth more than any single burst of brilliance. Years later, standing on the Olympic podium in Rio, he looked for his coach first.

A Filipino father would recognize this dynamic immediately. The son who wants to run ahead. The father who says: not yet, not that way. The instruction offered not as a leash but as a map — drawn by someone who has already walked the territory and knows where the ground gives way. We know this scene from both sides. And if we're honest, we know how often we've broken from the pace — pushed harder than we were meant to, let go of the hand, stumbled on ground we were warned about.

Three thousand years ago, a father sat with his son and said something that would have sounded familiar to Patrick Sang. But it was older. And it pointed toward something far bigger than any marathon.

Picture the scene: a man pulls his son close. Not to lecture from a distance but to lean in. Whatever the occasion — a departure, a transition, a moment the father knows won't come again — he speaks as if the words cost something. His voice is steady. His eyes are serious. He has earned the right to speak, and he is using it carefully.

"Listen, my son, accept what I say, and the years of your life will be many" (Proverbs 4:10, NIV). The opening word is everything: listen. Not "obey this command." Not "do what I say or else." Just: lean in, open up, receive. This is a father who understands that wisdom cannot be forced into a child — it has to be welcomed. And so he invites before he instructs.

"I instruct you in the way of wisdom and lead you along straight paths" (v. 11, NIV). He is handing over something real — a map made from his own feet. Every straight path he offers his son was once a winding road he had to navigate himself. This is what experience does when it is generous: it shortens the road for the next person.

And then: "When you walk, your steps will not be hampered; when you run, you will not stumble" (v. 12, NIV). The terrain isn't gone. But the instruction makes it walkable. There is a difference between a life without obstacles and a life with the right equipment. The father is offering the equipment.

Then comes the line that stops everything:

"Hold on to instruction, do not let it go; guard it well, for it is your life." — Proverbs 4:13 (NIV)

Notice what the father does not say. He doesn't say wisdom will make life easier. Doesn't promise the path gets flatter or that the road ahead gets friendly. He says something far stranger and more wonderful than all of that.

He says: this is your life.

Not "this will improve your life." Not "this is a good habit to develop." The instruction itself — the wisdom you hold, the word you guard — is the life. That is an extraordinary thing for a father to say. Worth pausing on. What kind of instruction carries that weight? Not a training plan. Not a family motto. Not even the wisest grandfather's best advice on its own.

Only one kind of teaching has ever made the audacious claim that it is life itself rather than merely pointing toward it.

Jesus said it plainly: "I am the way and the truth and the life" (John 14:6, NIV). Not "I know where life is." Not "I'll help you find it." I am. The writer of Proverbs, guided by God's Spirit, was leaning forward into this moment centuries before it arrived. The instruction the father presses into his son's hands isn't just wisdom about life — it points toward a Person who is life. The urgent, leaning-close voice of the Proverbs father echoes a Father's voice that would one day say: follow me, and you will have life.

Wisdom isn't just a set of principles. It's a relationship. And relationships have to be held.

Which brings us to the practical question — because Proverbs is nothing if not practical. What instruction are you holding right now? Or more honestly: what have you let slip?

Maybe it was your father's voice on the phone a year before he was gone. Something he said about patience, about not cutting corners, about what it means to be a person of your word. You nodded then. But somewhere between that conversation and this one, you set it down without meaning to.

Maybe it's a passage of Scripture that once undid you in the best possible way — that reshaped how you lived for an entire season. And then life accelerated, and the guarding stopped. You still remember the verse. But you've stopped holding it.

Maybe it's something your mentor said. Your pastor. Your lola, the last time you really talked. One line that you even wrote down somewhere. But you haven't gone back to it in months.

The father in Proverbs isn't asking his son to memorize a curriculum. He's asking him to guard it. That's a different word. You guard something valuable — something that could be taken, something whose absence you would grieve. Guard it well, he says. Not loosely. Not occasionally. Well.

Kipchoge did not become the greatest distance runner in history by following his coach when he felt motivated. He became extraordinary by holding to the instruction on ordinary mornings in the dark forests of Kaptagat, when no one was watching and his legs were arguing for a different pace. The discipline of the small, consistent day is what built the podium moment.

You have received instruction — real instruction, from the people God placed around you, from the Word He gave you, from the Spirit who still speaks. The question has never been whether you heard it.

The question is whether you're still holding it.

Hold on. Do not let it go. Guard it well. For the believer, the instruction isn't just a path to life — it is a lifeline back to the One who is life. And the years ahead, however many God gives, will be shaped by how faithfully you hold what you have already been given.

Today's practice doesn't need to be elaborate. Just go back to the instruction you already know is true — the one that once changed how you walked. Pick it up. Hold it close.

And guard it well.
